content.problemStatement:
A sequence of NNN integers is called a permutation if it contains all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.

For a permutation PPP of length NNN (where NNN is an **even integer**), we define the function F(P)F(P)F(P) as:

F(P)=F(P) = F(P)= (P1⊕P2)+(P3⊕P4)+…+(Pn−1⊕Pn)(P_1 \oplus P_2)  +  (P_3 \oplus P_4) +  \ldots  + (P_{n-1} \oplus P_n)(P1​⊕P2​)+(P3​⊕P4​)+…+(Pn−1​⊕Pn​). Here, ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

For Example: If P=[1,3,4,2]P=[1,3,4,2]P=[1,3,4,2] then F(P)=(1⊕3)+(4⊕2)=8F(P) = (1 \oplus 3)  +  (4 \oplus 2) = 8F(P)=(1⊕3)+(4⊕2)=8.

Given an **even integer** NNN, construct a permutation PPP of length NNN, such that F(P)F(P)F(P) is minimum.

If multiple such permutations exist, output any one of them.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains one **even** integer NNN - the length of the permutation to be constructed.


Output Format
For each test case, print **any** permutation PPP of length NNN such that F(P)F(P)F(P) is minimum.
constraints: Σ N over all test cases ≤ 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single even integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a permutation of length N (numbers 1 … N each exactly once) on one line, separated by spaces, such that the value F(P) = Σ (P_{2i-1} ⊕ P_{2i}) is minimum. Any permutation achieving the minimum is accepted.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=4 the permutation 1 4 2 3 gives pairs (1,4) and (2,3) with XORs 5 and 1, sum = 6 which is the minimum (3·4/2). For N=2 the only possible permutations give XOR 3, which equals the minimum (3·2/2).

solution.approach:
The sum Σ (a ⊕ b) is minimized when each pair (a,b) differs only in the least‑significant bit, because then a ⊕ b = 1. For the set {1,…,N} we can achieve this for all numbers except 1 and N: pair (2,3), (4,5), …, (N‑2, N‑1). The remaining numbers 1 and N form the last pair; since N is even, 1 ⊕ N = N+1. The total minimal sum is (N/2‑1)·1 + (N+1) = 3N/2. A concrete permutation respecting these pairs is: 1, N, 2, 3, 4, 5, …, N‑2, N‑1. The algorithm builds this sequence in O(N) time per test case.

Algorithm:
1. Read N.
2. Initialise an empty list ans.
3. Append 1 and N to ans.
4. For i from 2 to N‑1 stepping by 2, append i then i+1.
5. Output ans.
The construction works for N=2 as well (the loop is skipped, yielding "1 2").
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output array of each test case.
